The reason this is a complicated check is that we want to accept any channel being entirely 0 (in the `ndim >= 2` case). Hence, the addition of the `np.sum(reference_sources, axis=-1)` which sums all the channels for the `ndim >= 2` case. Since the trailing dimension for `ndim >= 2` is the channels, a check of trailing dimension all-zeros will almost always be true (one sample has zeros on both channels).

In my experiencing maintaining Django, we've recommended to use `git config core.excludesfile` for excluding IDE-specific files. That way if a new IDE comes along, its users can update their configuration own rather than requiring many projects to update `.gitingore`. I was also suggesting to exclude unrelated changes like this in a bug fix as that can be confusing when looking at `git blame`.

As an aside, this isn't my pet project, I just asked Sean this week if I could help him maintain it as it seems inactive.

There is no cache during runtime. Every container is starting from a clean state - the only actions that are being cached are the ones defined in the Dockerfiles. This means that every time you call ```Pkg.update()```, it will download and install the dependencies and throw everything away after the run has been finished. Any dependency management should happen in the Dockerfile (or the underlying install shellscripts).

I'm not sure this logic still works here, since now the only TI's being considered are from a single DagRun.

Let's say there are two DagRuns with a `depends_on_past=True` for the first task. The first DagRun will run normally. I think the second DagRun will report that it has unfinished tasks but that they can't run due to `depends_on_past` and it will deadlock. 

I think we should stop checking for that type of Deadlock, for simplicity. The deadlock in line 3489 will check if just this DAG is deadlocked, though it will do it in a way that respects the weird behavior of depends_on_past.

This will break every single Assimp client in existence, and require non-trivial changes when upgrading. Ideally this kind of optimization would preserve the public-facing struct API in some fashion, otherwise it'll probably have to wait until the next major version bump.

I took a stab at something similar a while ago in an attempt to minimize heap usage (https://github.com/assimp/assimp/compare/master...jdduke:noheap_face). It preserves the outward-facing aiFace struct data members, but eliminates heap allocations for faces with index count <= 4 (albeit in a somewhat crude fashion, index storage on the mesh is a better long term solution, but unless we make mIndices a pointer into the mesh index array, which has its own set of issues, this is a breaking change).

``__test__`` is a property that you can set to ensure test runners do not actually run the test. The BaseDownloadTest and its parent class have this set to ``False`` so we need to set it back to ``True``. I set it to ``False`` originally because it allows us to write a shared test case and all you have to do is update some of the properties based on the class of tests you are trying to run. In the case for the Base test classes these properties may not completely filled out so we should not be running/relying on them directly.

I removed it because I added the `choices` field to enforce only the filesystems we allow. If you provide a bad one now, it will show:

```
$ ceph-deploy disk prepare --fs-type zfs
usage: ceph-deploy disk prepare [-h] [--zap-disk] [--fs-type FS_TYPE]
                                [--dmcrypt] [--dmcrypt-key-dir KEYDIR]
                                HOST:DISK [HOST:DISK ...]
ceph-deploy disk prepare: error: argument --fs-type: invalid choice: 'zfs' (choose from 'xfs', 'ext4', 'btrfs')
```

A more standard approach to this is to set the value of `long_description` to the contents of a `README.md` (or similar) prepended to the contents of the `CHANGELOG.md` (or similar).

e.g.

```
with open('README.md') as _file:
  README = _file.read()
with open('CHANGELOG.md') as _file:
  CHANGELOG = _file.read()

setup(
  ...
  long_description=README + '\n\n# Change Log: \n' + CHANGELOG
  ...
)
```

> I much prefer always instantiating them. I'm convinced that the power of having instances rather than static class types will prove its point eventually.. and having a vague concept like "some hooks are instantiated and others are not" is dangerous and confusing.

TL;DR: I am suggesting that we simply replace the use of `__init__` with a differently-named class method. Functionally it would be the same. What do you think?

I am not proposing a vague alternative where sometimes hooks are instantiated and sometimes they are not. I am suggesting not instantiating any hooks ever. I agree that it's nice and powerful to be able to control certain events in a hook's lifecycle. I find using `__init__` specifically for this unintuitive. There is no real difference as far as I can tell from one instance of a hook class to another. All the methods could without modification be class methods. So let's not deal with instantiation at all -- it's sort of a "loaded" method.

You can the acs/_params.py file to define what install_location should be.
This means that when this method is called, the right install_location will be passed in.

So for example, you can remove the install locations that have been defined here and add the following lines to the `acs/_params.py` file:
```
import platform

def _get_default_install_location():
    system = platform.system()
    if system == 'Windows':
        return 'C:\Program Files\dcos.exe'
    if system == 'Linux':
        return '/usr/local/bin/dcos'
    if system == 'Darwin':
        return '/usr/local/bin/dcos'
    else:
        return None

register_cli_argument('acs dcos install', 'install_location', options_list=('--install-location',),
                      default=_get_default_install_location())
```

This means when the user runs `az acs dcos install -h`, they'll see:
```
Arguments
    --install-location                                        : Default: /usr/local/bin/dcos.
```
with the default being computed automatically.
